title: The Atlas Vampire of Stockholm 1932 🕯
segments: On May 4, 1932, in the Atlas neighborhood of Stockholm, neighbors noticed a foul smell coming from an apartment at Sankt Eriksplan. They hadn't seen the resident, Lilly Lindeström, in several days. Her friend Minnie Jansson was the one who called the police. What officers found inside has never been fully explained.
Lilly was 32. She worked as a sex worker in the area. Friends knew her routine. She had told Minnie on May 1 that she was expecting a client that evening and had asked to borrow a condom.
When officers entered the apartment, they found Lilly's body lying face-down across the bed. She had been struck multiple times in the head with a blunt object. She had been dead for three days.
What made the scene unusual was not just the violence. Beside the bed was a soup ladle stained with blood. Lilly had been drained of much of her blood, and a portion of it was missing entirely.
Stockholm police found no fingerprints. No murder weapon was recovered. The killer had washed himself in the apartment, dried himself with a towel, and arranged the body in a deliberate position.

Forensic analysis available in 1932 suggested that the killer had used the ladle to consume some of the victim's blood. Swedish newspapers reporting the case quickly nicknamed the killer 'Atlasvampyren' — the Atlas Vampire.

Here's where the case gets stranger. The autopsy revealed signs the killer had bitten Lilly during or after the attack. The pattern of bite marks was distinct, but odontology in the 1930s could not preserve usable evidence the way modern dental forensics can.

Stockholm investigators interviewed nearly every man known to have visited Lilly at her apartment. Many had already left the country. Her client list was, as one detective put it, 'long and difficult to verify.'

Lilly's friend Minnie was a key witness. She told police that Lilly had spoken about a regular visitor she described only as 'unusual.' Minnie did not know his name. She had never seen his face clearly.

Police canvassed local taverns, hotels, and harborside establishments. Sailors regularly passed through Stockholm, and detectives suspected the killer might have boarded a ship and left Sweden within days of the murder.

In the months that followed, Swedish papers covered the case daily. The 'vampire' angle drew attention across Europe. London newspapers picked up the story. Berlin tabloids ran the photograph of the apartment building.
The Stockholm police investigation gradually went quiet. By 1933 the file had moved to inactive status. There were no fingerprints. No DNA technology existed. Witness leads had run dry.

Then a new angle emerged in the late 1930s. A Swedish criminologist named Sture Ribbing reviewed the file and noted similarities between the Atlas case and unsolved attacks on women in Hamburg and Bremen. The geographic spread suggested a maritime worker.
Ribbing's theory drew interest. But the Second World War overwhelmed European cooperation. Files in Hamburg were destroyed during Allied bombing. The Stockholm case was effectively orphaned.

The original case file from 1932 still exists in the Swedish National Archives. It includes the autopsy report, the photographs of the apartment, the inventory of items in the bedroom, and several signed witness statements.
In the 1990s, Swedish researchers reopened analysis of the original photographs. They confirmed that the bite marks visible on Lilly's skin were inflicted at or near the time of death.
DNA technology cannot help. The biological evidence from the scene was not preserved. Items washed by the killer left no usable material. The body was buried more than 90 years ago.

What remains is a behavioral profile. Modern criminologists have suggested the killer was likely a man between 25 and 40, with previous violent fantasies, possibly with hematolagnia — a paraphilia involving blood. The deliberate use of the ladle is striking.

Some researchers argue the killer was probably a sailor on a long-distance route, since no similar Stockholm killings followed. Others suggest he was a local who suppressed his urges for the rest of his life.
The apartment building at Sankt Eriksgatan still stands. Locals know its history. Walking-tour guides occasionally stop near the front door and tell visitors the basic facts of the case.

Lilly Lindeström was buried in Stockholm. Her grave is unmarked in the original cemetery records, a common practice in 1932 for women in her profession. Local volunteers in recent years have placed flowers each May.

The Atlas Vampire case is one of the oldest documented unsolved murders in Sweden. It is also one of the most studied. Swedish criminology students still review the file as a case in early forensic limitations.
It is not a comfortable case. The brutality, the deliberate consumption, the absence of resolution — they sit with anyone who reads the original police report.
If you are researching pre-war Scandinavian crime, the Stockholm City Archives accept formal requests to view the Lilly Lindeström file under standard heritage research rules.
